equivalent_reason: Python normalises encoding names before looking them up in its codec registry. `"utf-8"` and `"UTF-8"` both resolve to the canonical name `"utf-8"`, making `Path.read_text(encoding="utf-8")` and `Path.read_text(encoding="UTF-8")` completely interchangeable. There is no observable difference in return value, exception type, or side effect, so no test can kill this mutant.

Removing `encoding="utf-8"` from `read_text()` while keeping `errors="ignore"` does not change observable behavior on any platform where Python's default locale encoding is UTF-8 (all modern Linux, macOS, and Windows CI environments). Python 3.15+ even makes UTF-8 mode the universal default. Since `errors="ignore"` is present in both versions, any bytes that would cause a decoding error are silently discarded regardless of which encoding is used. No test running in a standard environment can observe a difference between these two calls.

Python's `codecs` module normalises encoding names by lowercasing and removing hyphens/underscores before lookup. Both `"utf-8"` and `"UTF-8"` map to the same underlying UTF-8 codec. The existing test `test_sphinx_conf_reads_file_with_utf8_encoding` already handles this gracefully by comparing with `.casefold()`, confirming the project itself does not treat the case of the encoding string as observable. There is no way to write a test that passes with `"utf-8"` and fails with `"UTF-8"` (or vice-versa) because at runtime the two are indistinguishable.

On any system where `locale.getpreferredencoding(False)` returns `"UTF-8"` (all modern Linux/macOS defaults), passing `encoding=None` is functionally identical to `encoding="utf-8"`. The function only searches for the ASCII substrings `"apiVersion:"` and `"kind:"`, so the decoded content is always the same. The `errors="ignore"` parameter is unchanged in the mutant, and the mutation does not alter the control flow, return value, or any observable side effect for any realistic input.

The strings being searched for (`apiVersion:` and `kind:`) are pure ASCII and are therefore unaffected by the choice of encoding. With `errors="ignore"` still present, decoding failures under any encoding are silently suppressed. On any platform where the default locale encoding is UTF-8 (the standard on modern Linux, macOS, and Windows 10+), the two calls are byte-for-byte identical in behavior. No observable difference in return value, side effects, or exceptions can be produced by a test that does not explicitly manipulate the process locale.

Python encoding names are case-insensitive by specification. `"UTF-8"` and `"utf-8"` resolve to the same `codecs.CodecInfo` object, so `file.read_text(encoding="UTF-8", errors="ignore")` is functionally identical to `file.read_text(encoding="utf-8", errors="ignore")`. The function's return value, exception behaviour, and every downstream observable effect are the same for both spellings. A test that checks the exact string passed to `read_text` would be testing an irrelevant implementation detail (string casing), not real behaviour, and mutmut is not designed to be killed by such mock-based encoding-string checks when the actual codec is identical.

The `"repos"` exclusion in the `non_repo` comprehension is a redundant guard. `_deep_merge_top_level` (line 93) already contains `if key == "repos": continue`, which prevents the `repos` key from ever being written into the result dict during the top-level merge. Therefore, passing `repos` through to `_deep_merge_top_level` (as the mutant does) has no effect on the output. The repos data follows the separate `_merge_repos` path and is assigned to `result["repos"]` after the loop (lines 147-148), completely independent of the `non_repo` filter. Verified empirically: running both the original and mutant implementations on a preset with a `repos` key produces byte-for-byte identical output.

Even when the mutant allows "repos" to pass into non_repo, it is still silently dropped inside _deep_merge_top_level by the existing `if key == "repos": continue` guard. The repos list is still built correctly through the dedicated _merge_repos path and inserted at the end. No observable difference exists between the mutant and the original code for any input.

Both `[]` (original) and `None` (mutant) are falsy in Python. The code immediately checks `if primary` before using `primary` in any further operation. When the `primary_languages` key is absent from the preset dict, both defaults cause the `if` body to be skipped, resulting in the same outcome: no message is appended. There is no observable behavioral difference between the two values because the only use of `primary` after assignment is gated behind this truthiness check.

When `primary_languages` is absent from the preset dict, the original code returns `[]` and the mutant returns `None`. Both values are falsy. The immediately following `if primary and not any(...)` condition short-circuits to `False` for both values without evaluating `any(...)`, so no message is appended and no `TypeError` is raised. The return value of `get_primary_languages_info` is identical in every call scenario, making this mutation undetectable by any test.

PyYAML 6.x sets `default_flow_style=False` by default in its `Dumper` class (`yaml.Dumper(None).default_flow_style` is `False`). Removing the explicit `default_flow_style=False` from `yaml.dump(...)` therefore changes nothing - the rendered YAML output is byte-for-byte identical for all inputs tested (nested dicts, lists, unicode, long strings, empty dicts, etc.). No test can detect this mutation because there is no observable behavioural difference.

PyYAML's `yaml.dump_all` declares `sort_keys=True` as its default (visible in the function signature `def dump_all(..., sort_keys=True)`). The explicit `sort_keys=True` in the source code is therefore redundant; dropping it leaves the same default in effect. All existing tests (including `test_keys_are_sorted_in_output` and `test_sort_keys_for_determinism`) pass identically against the mutant because the YAML output is byte-for-byte identical.

PyYAML always passes the `flow` argument explicitly when calling `increase_indent` internally (e.g. `increase_indent(flow=False, indentless=True)`). The default value of `flow` in the overriding method is never reached during normal dumping, so changing it from `False` to `True` has no effect on the rendered output. Manual verification with the monkey-patched mutant confirms identical output for representative inputs.

The `indentless` parameter in the overridden `increase_indent` signature exists only to match the parent class signature; its value is never read. The method body hard-codes `indentless=False` in the `super()` call, so the actual indentation behaviour is identical whether the default is `False` or `True`. The `ARG002` noqa suppression on the same line confirms this is an intentionally unused parameter. No test can observe any difference between the original and mutated code.

The `flow` parameter to `yaml.Emitter.increase_indent` only changes behaviour when `self.indent is None` (the very first indent push). In that branch, `flow=True` sets `self.indent = best_indent` while `flow=False` sets `self.indent = 0`. However, `render_yaml` always dumps a top-level Python dict with `default_flow_style=False`, so the emitter's first `increase_indent` call is always for the top-level block mapping (`flow=False`), which sets `self.indent = 0`. Every later call — including the `flow=True` calls emitted for scalars, flow sequences and flow mappings nested inside the block structure — occurs when `self.indent` is already an integer, so the `flow` value is ignored and only the `elif not indentless` branch executes. Dropping `flow=flow` therefore produces no observable difference in the rendered YAML for any dict-valued input passed to `render_yaml`.

The `yaml.Dumper.increase_indent` method signature is `def increase_indent(self, flow=False, indentless=False)`. The original code passes `indentless=False` explicitly, and the mutated code omits the argument entirely, causing `super()` to use its own default value of `False`. Both calls result in `indentless=False` being used inside `yaml.Dumper.increase_indent`, producing identical YAML output. No observable behavioral difference exists between the two forms, so no test can kill this mutant.

PyYAML's `BaseRepresenter.__init__` signature is `def __init__(self, default_style=None, default_flow_style=False, sort_keys=True)`. The default value is already `False`, so passing `default_flow_style=False` explicitly is redundant. Removing it leaves the dumper with exactly the same configuration, and the YAML output is byte-for-byte identical. The existing test `test_output_uses_block_style_not_flow_style` does not kill the mutant because the mutated code still produces block-style output.

This mutation has no observable effect because the custom `_semantic_dict_representer` always calls `dumper.represent_mapping(tag, [(k, data[k]) for k in ordered])` with a **list** of pairs rather than a dict. Inside PyYAML's `represent_mapping`, the `sort_keys` re-sorting path is guarded by `if hasattr(mapping, 'items'):`, which is `False` for a list. Therefore, `sort_keys=True` (the default) never re-sorts the keys the representer has already ordered, and the YAML output is identical regardless of whether `sort_keys=False` is present.

The `_PrecommitDumper` class registers `_semantic_dict_representer` as the custom representer for all `dict` objects. This representer calls `dumper.represent_mapping()` with an explicitly pre-ordered list of `(key, value)` pairs — it never delegates key ordering back to yaml.dump. The `sort_keys` parameter in `yaml.dump` only affects the default (non-custom-representer) mapping serialization path. Because every dict in the output is serialized through the custom representer, `sort_keys=True` and `sort_keys=False` produce byte-for-byte identical YAML output, making the mutant undetectable by any test.

Python's built-in `open()` (and `Path.open()`) normalises encoding names case-insensitively via the `codecs` module. `"utf-8"` and `"UTF-8"` resolve to the same codec and produce byte-for-byte identical file reads. No caller can observe any difference in return value, raised exceptions, or side effects between the two forms. The existing test at `tests/unit/test_loader.py:108` explicitly uses `.casefold()` on the captured encoding string to accept both variants, confirming that the test authors considered the casing irrelevant to correctness.

Python normalizes encoding identifiers: `codecs.lookup("UTF-8").name` returns `"utf-8"`, the same canonical name as `codecs.lookup("utf-8").name`. Passing `encoding="UTF-8"` to `Path.open()` is functionally identical to `encoding="utf-8"` — the file is read with the same codec, producing the same bytes-to-str decoding. No test can distinguish the two because there is no runtime difference in behaviour, file content, exceptions raised, or return values.

Every call to `_generate_content` in the codebase always passes the `recommended` argument explicitly (`recommended=recommended`). The default value of the parameter (`False` or `True`) is never exercised by any code path, so changing it from `False` to `True` produces no observable difference in behaviour.

The `_run` function is only called from `_dispatch` (line 163: `_run(langs, frameworks, target, base_dir, recommended=recommended)`), which always passes `recommended` explicitly as a keyword argument. The default value of the parameter is therefore dead code — it is never invoked. Changing the default from `False` to `True` does not alter any observable behaviour, making this an equivalent mutant.

The function `_dispatch` is called exactly once in the entire codebase (gpc_init/cli.py line 419), and that call always supplies `recommended=recommended` as a keyword argument. The default value of the parameter is therefore dead code — it can never be reached. Changing the default from `False` to `True` produces identical observable behaviour in every possible execution path, making the mutant equivalent.

`_handle_existing_file` has exactly one call site in the entire codebase (`_dispatch`, line 159), and that call always passes `recommended=recommended` explicitly. Because the default value is never relied upon by any caller, changing it from `False` to `True` produces identical runtime behavior for every reachable code path. There is no way to write a test that calls `_handle_existing_file` through the CLI and exercises the default value, because `_dispatch` always supplies it.

The mutant status was "not checked" (never executed), not "survived". After running `mutmut run "gpc_init.cli.x__handle_existing_file__mutmut_5"` the mutant was immediately marked "killed". The existing test `TestDiffOnExistingFile::test_diff_no_changes_with_custom_presets_uses_custom_presets_for_comparison` in `tests/integration/test_cli_generation.py` (lines 1228–1260) already covers this exact scenario: it generates a file with a custom `--presets` directory, then invokes the CLI again with the same custom presets (no `--force`), and asserts `exit_code == 0` plus `"No changes"` in output. With the mutant applied, the comparison regenerates content from the bundled catalog instead of the custom dir, producing a spurious diff and exit code 1, which fails the assertion. No new test is needed.

Python's `codecs.lookup` normalises codec names by lowercasing and replacing hyphens/spaces, so `"UTF-8"` and `"utf-8"` both resolve to the same codec object. `Path.read_text` passes the encoding argument directly to the built-in `open()`, which applies the same normalisation. The two calls are therefore functionally identical on all supported Python versions and operating systems, making this mutation undetectable by any test.

The mutant is not semantically equivalent but is already caught by the existing test `TestDiffOnExistingFile::test_force_command_hint_uses_comma_separator_for_multiple_frameworks` in `/home/christian/devel/private/gpc-init/tests/integration/test_cli_generation.py`. When the mutant is applied, that test's assertion `assert "--framework=django,react" in result.output` fails because `_build_force_command(langs, None)` omits the `--framework` clause from the hint. The mutmut status "not checked" means the mutant was never evaluated against the test suite (likely added after the last full mutmut run), not that it actually survives. No new test is needed.

The mutant gpc_init.cli.x__handle_existing_file__mutmut_51 has never existed in any version of the source code. Running mutmut's mutation engine against every historical commit of gpc_init/cli.py confirms the function `_handle_existing_file` generates at most 50 mutations (numbered 1-50). Mutant 51 was never produced, so it cannot alter any observable behaviour — there is no code change to detect.

Python's `importlib.metadata.version()` implements PEP 503 package name normalization: it lowercases the name and collapses `[-_.]` sequences before the filesystem lookup. Therefore `version("PC-INIT")` resolves to exactly the same distribution as `version("pc-init")` and returns the same version string. No test can observe a difference in the output or exit code, making this mutant equivalent. The existing `TestVersion` tests mock `importlib.metadata.version` entirely, so they never exercise the real lookup argument at all.
